explain: EC2 provides resizable virtual machines where you can select instance types, OS, and applications. Auto Scaling automatically adds/removes instances based on demand.

q: A team runs data analytics requiring high CPU and memory. Which EC2 instance family is optimized for this?
options: I-family for storage-optimized workloads
C-family for compute-optimized workloads with high CPU
R-family for memory-optimized workloads
T-family for burstable workloads
explain: EC2 instance families are optimized: T (burstable general use), C (compute-optimized, high CPU), R (memory-optimized), I (storage-optimized). Choosing the right family optimizes price/performance.

q: A company has a steady, long-term application. What EC2 pricing model minimizes cost?
options: Reserved Instances (1 or 3-year commitment) for 30-70% discounts
Dedicated Hosts for specific licensing
On-Demand pricing for maximum flexibility
Spot Instances for 70-90% discount but can be interrupted
explain: Reserved Instances offer 30-70% discounts for 1 or 3-year commitments. For steady, long-term workloads, RIs provide savings while maintaining flexibility.

q: Which disaster recovery strategy offers the fastest recovery time but highest cost, by keeping a fully operational duplicate environment always running?
options: Multi-Site Active/Active — full production capacity in multiple regions simultaneously
Pilot Light — minimal critical systems always running, scale up when needed
Backup and Restore — cheapest but slowest, restore from backup when needed
Warm Standby — scaled-down but fully functional version always running
explain: Multi-Site Active/Active runs full production in multiple AWS regions simultaneously. Failover is instant (near-zero RTO/RPO) but costs 2x since you run full infrastructure in multiple regions. Most expensive DR strategy.

q: A company wants the cheapest disaster recovery strategy and can tolerate several hours of downtime. Which approach should they use?
options: Pilot Light with only core database systems running
Multi-Site Active/Active for zero downtime
Warm Standby with a scaled-down live environment
Backup and Restore — back up data to S3 and restore EC2 instances when needed
explain: Backup and Restore is the cheapest DR strategy: back up data to S3 or AMI snapshots. During disaster, provision new resources from scratch. Recovery takes hours but costs near zero when not in use.

q: Which AWS service helps track the cost of AWS resources by assigning metadata labels to resources for cost allocation?
options: Cost allocation tags identifying and tracking resource costs by project or team
AWS Budgets setting spending alerts
AWS Cost Explorer analyzing spending patterns
AWS Trusted Advisor recommending cost optimizations
explain: Cost allocation tags are key-value pairs (e.g., Project=Analytics, Team=Engineering) attached to AWS resources. After activation in Billing, they appear in Cost Explorer and detailed billing reports for granular cost tracking.

q: A company wants to serve static website assets globally with low latency. Which AWS service is designed for this?
options: AWS Lambda for serverless websites
Amazon RDS for database-backed content
EC2 instances in multiple regions
Amazon CloudFront — a CDN caching content at edge locations worldwide
explain: CloudFront is AWS's CDN. It caches content at 400+ edge locations globally, serving cached content from locations nearest users, reducing latency dramatically.

q: Which AWS Support plan is the minimum required to get 24/7 phone and chat access to Cloud Support Engineers?
options: Enterprise Support — dedicated TAM plus 24/7 support
Business Support — 24/7 phone and chat with Cloud Support Engineers
Basic Support — free but limited to documentation and forums
Developer Support — business hours email support only
explain: Business Support (from $100/month) is the minimum plan with 24/7 phone and chat access to AWS Cloud Support Engineers. Developer Support only provides business hours email. Enterprise adds a dedicated TAM.

q: What response time SLA does Business Support provide for production system down cases?
options: 24 hours — next business day response
4 hours — urgent response for impaired production systems
12 hours — same day business hours response
1 hour — for production system down cases
explain: Business Support guarantees: <1 hour for production system DOWN, <4 hours for production IMPAIRED, <12 hours for general guidance. Enterprise Support offers <15 minutes for business-critical systems down.

q: Which AWS tool provides automated recommendations across cost, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits?
options: AWS Config checking configuration compliance against rules
Amazon CloudWatch monitoring metrics and setting alarms
AWS Trusted Advisor providing real-time best practice recommendations
AWS Well-Architected Tool for structured architecture review
explain: AWS Trusted Advisor inspects your AWS environment and provides actionable recommendations in 5 categories: Cost Optimization, Performance, Security, Fault Tolerance, and Service Limits. Business/Enterprise plans get full checks.

q: An application needs to store objects like files, images, and videos. Which AWS service is optimized for this?
options: Amazon S3 for object storage with unlimited scalability
Amazon DynamoDB for NoSQL tables
Amazon RDS for structured databases
Amazon EC2 for compute and storage
explain: Amazon S3 is an object storage service optimized for files and unstructured data. It offers unlimited scalability, high durability, and integrates with many AWS services.

q: A company archives data rarely accessed. Which S3 storage class optimizes for long-term archival?
options: S3 Standard for frequently accessed data
S3 One Zone-IA for single AZ infrequent access
S3 Intelligent-Tiering for automatic optimization
S3 Glacier or S3 Glacier Deep Archive for archival with very low cost
explain: S3 Glacier/Glacier Deep Archive offer the lowest costs for long-term archives but require retrieval time (minutes to hours/days).

q: Which cloud deployment model uses dedicated hardware for a single organization, hosted and managed by a third-party provider?
options: On-premises private cloud — owned and operated by the organization
Hosted private cloud — dedicated hardware managed by a third party
Public cloud — multi-tenant shared AWS infrastructure
Hybrid cloud — combining on-premises with public cloud
explain: Hosted private cloud uses dedicated (single-tenant) infrastructure but the hardware is hosted and managed by a provider like AWS Outposts or managed hosting companies. Combines dedicated resources with managed operations.

q: A developer uses Amazon S3 and wants to prevent accidental deletion of critical objects. Which S3 feature provides this?
options: S3 Object Lock — protects objects from deletion/overwrite for compliance (WORM)
Deletion is always immediate
S3 has no deletion protection
Only S3 Glacier provides protection
explain: S3 Object Lock enables Write Once Read Many (WORM) mode, preventing objects from being deleted or overwritten even by root account. Essential for compliance archives.

q: Which pricing model allows customers to commit to a consistent compute usage amount in exchange for up to 72% discount vs On-Demand?
options: Dedicated Hosts — pay for a physical server dedicated to your use
Spot Instances — bid for unused EC2 capacity at up to 90% discount
Reserved Instances or Savings Plans — commit to usage for 1 or 3 years for discount
On-Demand — pay for compute by the hour with no commitment
explain: Reserved Instances (1 or 3 year commitment) and Savings Plans offer up to 72% discount vs On-Demand in exchange for usage commitment. Best for steady-state workloads with predictable resource requirements.

q: A company is migrating and wants AWS to help identify which applications to move first. Which service provides migration assessment and planning?
options: AWS Database Migration Service for moving databases
AWS Schema Conversion Tool for database migration
AWS Migration Hub for tracking migrations across services
AWS Application Discovery Service gathering on-premises data for migration planning
explain: AWS Application Discovery Service collects data about on-premises servers (performance, dependencies, configurations) to help plan migrations. It feeds into Migration Hub for tracking migration progress across DMS and SMS.

q: A company needs a managed relational database without managing software or hardware. Which service fits?
options: Amazon S3 for structured data
Amazon RDS — managed relational database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, Aurora)
EC2 instances with self-managed MySQL
Amazon DynamoDB for NoSQL only
explain: RDS is a managed service: AWS handles patches, backups, failover, replication, and maintenance. You define instance type and storage; AWS handles the rest.

q: A startup evaluates Aurora versus traditional RDS. What is Aurora's primary advantage?
options: Suitable only for NoSQL workloads
Requires manual database administration
Cheaper than RDS
Up to 5x faster than RDS with automatic scaling and high availability
explain: Aurora is AWS's proprietary relational database optimized for performance (5x MySQL speed) and cost, with automatic scaling and high availability.

q: Which Well-Architected pillar focuses on the ability of a workload to perform its intended function correctly and consistently, and recover from failures?
options: Operational Excellence — running and improving operations
Security — protecting information and systems
Reliability — recovering from failures and meeting demand
Performance Efficiency — using resources efficiently
explain: The Reliability pillar covers: automatic recovery from failure, testing recovery procedures, horizontal scaling, stopping guessing capacity, and managing change through automation. Key services: Route 53, RDS Multi-AZ, Auto Scaling.

q: A company runs batch processing jobs triggered on-demand. Which service optimizes cost?
options: Running EC2 instances 24/7
AWS Lambda — serverless compute triggered on-demand, paying per invocation
Amazon ECS for container orchestration
AWS Batch only
explain: Lambda is ideal for event-driven, short-duration jobs: no servers to manage, pay per invocation (first 1 million free/month), scales automatically.

q: Which AWS service helps customers move petabyte-scale data to AWS when internet transfer would take too long?
options: AWS DataSync for online data transfer via internet or Direct Connect
AWS Snowball Edge — physical device that ships to your data center for offline data transfer
Amazon Kinesis for streaming real-time data to AWS
AWS Transfer Family for SFTP-based data transfer
explain: AWS Snow family (Snowball, Snowmobile) provides physical devices for offline data transfer. Snowball Edge handles 80TB per device — faster and more cost-effective than internet transfer for large datasets.

q: What does the 'Sustainability' pillar (6th pillar) of the AWS Well-Architected Framework focus on?
options: Long-term business sustainability and profitability in the cloud
Minimizing environmental impact by reducing energy consumption and carbon footprint
Ensuring workloads run sustainably without interruption
Sustainable pricing through long-term Reserved Instance commitments
explain: The Sustainability pillar (added 2021) focuses on minimizing environmental impact of cloud workloads: right-sizing resources, using graviton processors, optimizing code efficiency, and choosing regions powered by renewable energy.

q: A team discusses 'serverless architecture.' What does 'serverless' mean for AWS Lambda?
options: AWS manages servers: developers write functions; AWS handles provisioning, scaling, patching
All AWS services are serverless
Code runs without the internet
No servers are involved
explain: Serverless doesn't mean no servers; it means AWS manages servers. Developers focus on function code, not infrastructure.

q: Which AWS program lets startups get AWS credits, training, and support to build their businesses on AWS?
options: AWS Marketplace for purchasing startup software solutions
AWS Free Tier for new accounts experimenting with services
AWS Migration Acceleration Program for enterprise migrations
AWS Activate providing credits, training, and support specifically for startups
explain: AWS Activate provides startups with AWS credits (up to $100,000+), technical training, business support, and access to startup-focused resources through accelerators, incubators, and VC partners.

q: A company needs a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). What are the primary VPC components?
options: Subnets (public/private), Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, Route Tables, Security Groups, NACLs
Only EC2 instances
VPCs don't provide network isolation
Just a security group
explain: A VPC is a logically isolated network with subnets, gateways (Internet, NAT), routing, and firewalls (Security Groups, NACLs).

q: A financial company needs its AWS resources to only be accessible over a private dedicated network connection, not the public internet. Which service provides this?
options: VPC Peering connecting two VPCs privately
AWS Direct Connect — dedicated private fiber connection from data center to AWS
AWS PrivateLink for private connectivity to AWS services
VPN Gateway using encrypted tunnels over the internet
explain: AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated, private fiber connection from a customer's data center to AWS, bypassing the public internet. Ideal for consistent network performance, large data transfers, and compliance requirements.

q: What is the purpose of the AWS Service Health Dashboard vs AWS Personal Health Dashboard?
options: They are identical dashboards showing the same information
Service Health Dashboard is for enterprise customers; Personal Health Dashboard is for individual developers
Service Health Dashboard shows all AWS service status globally; Personal Health Dashboard shows issues affecting YOUR specific resources
Personal Health Dashboard costs extra; Service Health Dashboard is free
explain: Service Health Dashboard (status.aws.amazon.com) shows global AWS service status. Personal Health Dashboard (AWS Health) shows events specifically affecting your resources and provides proactive notifications for planned maintenance.

q: Private database instances need internet access for updates. How is this provided securely?
options: Private databases don't need updates
Private subnets cannot access the internet
Opening all ports to the internet
Using a NAT Gateway in the public subnet for outbound-only access
explain: NAT Gateway in the public subnet enables private instances to initiate outbound connections while blocking inbound internet traffic.

q: A company has multiple AWS accounts and needs a centralized place to manage all security findings. Which service provides this unified security view?
options: AWS Security Hub aggregating findings from multiple security services across accounts
Amazon GuardDuty for threat detection across accounts
IAM Access Analyzer analyzing resource permissions
AWS Config tracking compliance across all accounts
explain: AWS Security Hub provides a comprehensive view of security alerts and compliance status across AWS accounts, aggregating findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and partner tools into a prioritized security dashboard.

q: An architect designs DNS routing for global applications. Which service routes traffic geographically?
options: Amazon CloudFront only
AWS Lambda for serverless routing
Security Groups for routing
Amazon Route 53 — AWS's DNS with geolocation routing
explain: Route 53 provides DNS with intelligent routing: geolocation, latency-based, weighted, failover routing. This directs users to optimal endpoints automatically.

q: Which AWS service enables single sign-on for employees to access multiple AWS accounts and business applications with one set of credentials?
options: AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) for centralized single sign-on across accounts and applications
AWS IAM for individual user authentication
AWS Directory Service for Microsoft Active Directory integration
Amazon Cognito for customer-facing application authentication
explain: AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) provides centralized access management to multiple AWS accounts and cloud applications (Salesforce, Office 365). Employees sign in once using corporate credentials for all resources.

q: What is the difference between security groups and Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) in AWS?
options: Security groups are stateful instance-level firewalls; NACLs are stateless subnet-level firewalls with explicit allow and deny rules
NACLs are stricter and override security group settings
Security groups require more configuration; NACLs are automatically configured
Security groups are for databases; NACLs are for web servers
explain: Security groups: stateful (track connections), instance-level, allow rules only, evaluate all rules. NACLs: stateless (must allow return traffic), subnet-level, allow AND deny rules, process rules in number order. Both apply to VPC traffic.

q: What is AWS Shield Advanced and when would a company need it over Shield Standard?
options: Shield Advanced adds DDoS cost protection, attack visibility, response team access, and advanced attack mitigation for sophisticated DDoS attacks
Shield Standard for small companies; Shield Advanced only for enterprises
Shield Advanced provides WAF rules; Shield Standard does not
Shield Advanced is required for all production AWS workloads
explain: Shield Advanced ($3,000/month) adds: 24/7 DDoS Response Team access, real-time attack visibility, cost protection (credits for DDoS-related scaling costs), advanced detection, and protection for EC2, ELB, CloudFront, Route 53, and Global Accelerator.

q: Which AWS service helps prevent accidental public exposure of S3 buckets and other resources by analyzing resource policies?
options: AWS Config checking S3 bucket public access settings
IAM Access Analyzer identifying resources accessible from outside your account
Amazon GuardDuty detecting unauthorized S3 access
Amazon Macie scanning S3 for sensitive data exposure
explain: IAM Access Analyzer examines resource policies (S3, IAM roles, KMS keys, Lambda, SQS) to identify resources shared with external entities, helping find unintended public access or cross-account sharing.

q: Under the shared responsibility model, who is responsible for patching the operating system on an EC2 instance?
options: Neither — EC2 instances automatically patch themselves
Both AWS and customer share OS patching responsibility equally
The customer — EC2 is IaaS so customers manage the OS and above
AWS — they manage all software running on their infrastructure
explain: For EC2 (IaaS), customers own the OS and everything above: OS patches, application updates, runtime security. AWS manages the hypervisor and physical infrastructure. Contrast with RDS where AWS patches the database engine.

q: A company balances traffic across multiple web servers automatically. Which service distributes traffic?
options: EC2 Auto Scaling alone
Route 53 for DNS only
Security Groups for load balancing
AWS Elastic Load Balancing (ALB, NLB, CLB) — distributes traffic with health checks
explain: Elastic Load Balancing automatically distributes traffic across healthy instances, removes unhealthy targets, and scales. ALB (Layer 7), NLB (Layer 4), CLB (classic).

q: Which compliance framework is most relevant for companies storing healthcare data (PHI) in AWS?
options: ISO 27001 for information security management systems
SOC 2 for service organization security controls
PCI DSS for payment card data protection
HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) for protected health information
explain: HIPAA governs protected health information (PHI). AWS signs a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with healthcare customers and offers HIPAA-eligible services. Customers are responsible for HIPAA-compliant configurations.

q: What is 'encryption in transit' and which protocol implements it for web traffic?
options: Encrypting data on disk using AES-256 encryption
Encrypting data before it leaves your application server
Encrypting data as it travels across networks using TLS/SSL protocols
Compressing data for faster network transmission
explain: Encryption in transit (TLS/SSL) protects data moving between clients and servers, or between AWS services. AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) provides free SSL/TLS certificates for CloudFront, ALB, and API Gateway.

q: A company needs to ensure no one in their organization can create resources in regions outside of us-east-1 and eu-west-1. What is the best approach?
options: Configure AWS Config rules alerting on resources in other regions
Create IAM policies denying all other regions for each user
Use AWS Organizations SCPs to deny all actions in non-approved regions
Set up CloudTrail alarms for API calls in unauthorized regions
explain: SCPs in AWS Organizations can deny all actions in non-approved regions using Condition keys like aws:RequestedRegion. This creates an organization-wide guardrail that applies to all accounts, even administrators.

q: What AWS service continuously monitors and records AWS resource configurations, enabling compliance auditing and change tracking?
options: Amazon CloudWatch monitoring metrics and system performance
AWS Security Hub aggregating security compliance findings
AWS Config recording resource configurations and changes over time
AWS CloudTrail recording all API calls made in the account
explain: AWS Config continuously records resource configurations and changes, evaluates against compliance rules, and maintains a configuration history. Enables questions like 'what did this security group look like 30 days ago?'

q: A company stores data in RDS and wants automated failover if the primary database fails. What RDS feature enables this?
options: Manual failover is required
RDS Multi-AZ synchronously replicates data to a standby, automatically promoting the standby on failure
Only DynamoDB supports automatic failover
Data is never replicated in RDS
explain: RDS Multi-AZ provides high availability: synchronous replication to a standby database in another AZ with automatic failover on failure.

q: Which AWS service provides managed Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) protection and is automatically applied to all AWS customers at no charge?
options: Amazon GuardDuty — detecting volumetric attack patterns
AWS Shield Standard — automatic protection against network/transport layer DDoS at no cost
AWS Network Firewall — managed VPC firewall service
AWS WAF — web application firewall filtering layer 7 attacks
explain: AWS Shield Standard is included free for all AWS customers, providing automatic protection against the most common, frequently occurring network and transport layer DDoS attacks that target AWS services.

q: A company wants to ensure that all EC2 instances in their AWS account always have encrypted EBS volumes. What is the most efficient way to enforce this?
options: Use AWS Config rules to alert when unencrypted volumes are found
Create an IAM policy preventing creation of unencrypted volumes
Manually encrypt each EBS volume when launching instances
Enable default EBS encryption in the account settings to encrypt all new volumes automatically
explain: Account-level EBS encryption default ensures all newly created EBS volumes and snapshots are automatically encrypted using KMS, without requiring any action when launching instances. This is the simplest enforcement mechanism.

q: A company needs to alert teams when CPU usage exceeds 80%. Which AWS service provides this?
options: AWS CloudWatch — provides metrics, monitoring, and alarms
AWS Systems Manager for resource management
AWS Config for compliance tracking
AWS Trusted Advisor for cost optimization
explain: CloudWatch collects metrics from services and resources. Alarms trigger notifications when metrics exceed thresholds.

q: Which AWS service provides a fully managed network firewall for filtering traffic entering and leaving VPCs?
options: Network ACLs filtering subnet traffic
Security groups filtering EC2 instance traffic
AWS Network Firewall providing stateful deep packet inspection for VPCs
AWS WAF filtering HTTP traffic at the application layer
explain: AWS Network Firewall is a managed VPC firewall service providing stateful deep packet inspection, IPS (intrusion prevention), and URL filtering. Unlike security groups and NACLs, it supports Suricata-compatible rules for advanced threat detection.

q: A healthcare provider requires database high availability with automatic failover. Which service provides this?
options: No AWS service provides automatic failover
Self-managed MySQL on EC2
Amazon RDS with Multi-AZ deployment — automatic failover on failure
Amazon S3 for databases
explain: RDS Multi-AZ provides automatic failover: primary database is synchronously replicated to a standby in another AZ. On failure, the standby is promoted automatically.

q: A company must demonstrate to auditors that AWS meets specific compliance standards. What self-service AWS resource provides compliance documentation?
options: AWS Security Hub showing compliance scores and findings
AWS Trusted Advisor showing security compliance best practices
AWS Config providing configuration compliance reports
AWS Artifact providing on-demand AWS compliance reports, certifications, and agreements
explain: AWS Artifact is a self-service portal providing: AWS compliance reports (SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS, ISO), compliance agreements (HIPAA BAA, GDPR DPA), and security audit documentation for customer auditors.

q: A healthcare platform needs daily backups and point-in-time recovery. Which RDS feature enables this?
options: Manual snapshots only
RDS automated backups — continuous daily backups enabling point-in-time recovery up to 35 days
No automated backups available
S3 backup system
explain: RDS automated backups create daily snapshots retained for up to 35 days. Combined with transaction logs, this enables point-in-time recovery.

q: A company uses CloudFront to serve content globally. What primary benefit does it provide?
options: CloudFront is more expensive
CloudFront adds security
Direct S3 is faster than CloudFront
CloudFront caches content at edge locations globally, reducing latency and bandwidth costs
explain: CloudFront is a CDN caching content at 400+ edge locations. Users download from nearby edges instead of the origin, reducing latency significantly.

q: Which AWS service enables federated access to AWS using existing corporate identities from Microsoft Active Directory?
options: AWS IAM Identity Center for multi-account SSO
AWS Directory Service connecting AWS to Microsoft AD or running Managed AD in the cloud
AWS IAM creating native AWS user accounts
Amazon Cognito for customer application identity
explain: AWS Directory Service provides options: AD Connector (proxy to on-premises AD), AWS Managed Microsoft AD (fully managed AD in AWS), and Simple AD. Enables employees to use existing Windows credentials for AWS console access.

q: A startup doesn't have dedicated security staff. Which AWS service provides automated security recommendations based on best practices for free?
options: AWS Trusted Advisor Basic providing core security checks for free
AWS Security Hub aggregating security findings
Amazon Inspector running vulnerability assessments
Amazon GuardDuty providing ML-based threat detection
explain: AWS Trusted Advisor Basic (free) provides core security checks: S3 bucket permissions, security group open ports, IAM usage, MFA on root, public EBS snapshots, and public RDS snapshots. A quick automated security review available to all.

q: What are the S3 storage classes and which is most appropriate for frequently accessed data requiring millisecond access?
options: S3 Glacier — cheapest storage for frequently accessed data
S3 Intelligent-Tiering — automatically moves data between tiers
S3 Standard-IA — infrequent access tier for active data
S3 Standard — high durability, availability, and performance for frequently accessed data
explain: S3 Standard: 99.999999999% durability, 99.99% availability, millisecond access, no retrieval fee. Best for frequently accessed data like web assets, active datasets. More expensive than IA tiers but no retrieval charges.

q: A company stores compliance data that is rarely accessed but must be retrievable within 12 hours at minimal cost. Which S3 storage class is best?
options: S3 Standard — high performance but expensive for rarely accessed data
S3 Express One Zone — single-AZ for frequently accessed data
S3 Standard-IA — lower storage cost with retrieval fee for infrequent access
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval — low-cost archival with retrieval times from minutes to 12 hours
explain: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval (formerly Glacier) is for archival data: 90-day minimum storage, retrieval options from 1-5 minutes (Expedited), 3-5 hours (Standard), or 5-12 hours (Bulk). Far cheaper than S3 Standard for rarely accessed compliance data.

q: An architect designs an application requiring global low-latency content delivery. CloudFront is selected for the CDN. Why?
options: CloudFront only works in the US
Direct S3 is faster than CloudFront
CloudFront is expensive and slow
CloudFront caches content at 400+ edge locations globally, serving users from nearby locations
explain: CloudFront reduces latency by caching content globally at edge locations, serving users from nearby instead of making requests to the origin.

q: A startup needs to monitor applications for errors and performance. Which AWS service provides detailed insights?
options: AWS X-Ray — traces requests end-to-end, revealing bottlenecks and errors
CloudFront for CDN only
AWS CloudTrail for API logging only
Route 53 for DNS only
explain: AWS X-Ray provides distributed tracing: instruments applications to track requests end-to-end, showing latency, dependencies, and errors.

q: A company manages containers and needs orchestration. What AWS service provides this?
options: Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) or EKS (Kubernetes) for orchestration
EC2 instances only
S3 for container images
Lambda for all container workloads
explain: ECS is AWS's native container orchestration. EKS runs Kubernetes. Both handle deployment, scaling, and health checks.

q: A startup uses containers but wants to avoid managing infrastructure. Which service runs containers without managing servers?
options: S3 for container storage
EC2 instances directly
Amazon RDS
AWS Fargate — serverless containers using ECS or EKS
explain: Fargate is serverless container compute: define your container and resources; Fargate handles launching and scaling. You pay per container per second; no servers to manage.

q: A company wants to store container images securely. Which AWS service is a container registry?
options: S3 for all images
Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) — manages private images with built-in scanning
EC2 instances
CloudFront for images
explain: ECR is a fully managed container registry integrating with ECS and EKS. It stores private images, performs vulnerability scanning, and integrates with IAM.

q: A company processes high-volume data in batches. Which service is optimized for this?
options: Lambda for all batch jobs
CloudWatch for batch processing
AWS Batch — fully managed batch computing with automatic scaling
EC2 instances only
explain: AWS Batch manages batch computing: define jobs, Batch handles provisioning instances, scheduling, and scaling. Ideal for large-scale, long-running workloads.

q: A company with variable workloads wants flexibility without long-term commitments. Which pricing model fits?
options: Reserved Instances requiring commitments
Spot Instances with interruption risk
On-Demand pricing — pay per hour/second with flexibility to scale
Fixed pricing regardless of usage
explain: On-Demand pricing offers maximum flexibility: pay for capacity used, no commitments, scale instantly. More expensive per unit than RIs/Spot.

q: A company commits to a steady application for 3 years. What pricing model offers maximum savings?
options: On-Demand for flexibility
Spot Instances for interruption risk
3-year Reserved Instances for 60-72% discount
Pay-as-you-go without analysis
explain: 3-year RIs provide 60-72% discounts (all upfront, partial, or no upfront options). For steady workloads, RIs provide the best price-to-performance.

q: A startup wants to reduce EC2 costs and tolerates interruptions. What pricing option is ideal?
options: Reserved Instances
No cost optimization
Spot Instances — spare capacity at 70-90% discount, usable if interruptions are tolerable
On-Demand pricing
explain: Spot Instances provide up to 90% discount on spare AWS capacity but can be reclaimed with 2-minute notice. Ideal for fault-tolerant, batch workloads.

q: Which AWS service provides a petabyte-scale data warehouse for complex analytical queries across large datasets?
options: Amazon DynamoDB for high-speed NoSQL analytical queries
Amazon Aurora for high-performance relational analytics
Amazon RDS for relational database workloads
Amazon Redshift — managed petabyte-scale data warehouse using columnar storage
explain: Amazon Redshift is a managed data warehouse using columnar storage and massively parallel processing (MPP). Ideal for BI, analytics, and complex SQL queries across petabytes of structured data from multiple sources.

q: A company wants reduced costs without capacity commitment. What combines savings plans with flexibility?
options: AWS Savings Plans — hourly commitment discounts (up to 66%) across instance families and services
No flexible savings
Reserved Instances
On-Demand pricing only
explain: Savings Plans offer flexibility: commit to $/hour (not specific instances), apply across EC2 families, regions, Lambda, Fargate. 20-66% savings.

q: A startup launches on AWS and wants to minimize first-year costs. What is the AWS Free Tier?
options: AWS is always paid
Free tier is not available
12-month free tier, always-free services, and trials
All AWS services are free indefinitely
explain: AWS Free Tier provides: 12-month free (EC2, RDS, S3 limits), always-free (Lambda 1M invocations, DynamoDB 25 GB), and trials. Careful: overages are charged.

q: A company needs to forecast monthly costs before incurring them. Which AWS tool provides estimates?
options: AWS CloudWatch for monitoring only
AWS Cost Explorer for historical analysis only
AWS Pricing Calculator — estimates costs based on selected services
No cost estimation tools available
explain: AWS Pricing Calculator estimates costs: select services, configure parameters, and it calculates expected monthly costs. Helps budget before launch.

q: A company's AWS costs are increasing unexpectedly. Which service analyzes spending and identifies drivers?
options: AWS CloudWatch for metrics only
CloudTrail for API logging only
AWS Cost Explorer — visualizes spending, identifies drivers, and forecasts
AWS Budgets for cost limits only
explain: AWS Cost Explorer breaks down costs by service, account, usage type, and region. It shows trends and forecasts future spending.

q: A company wants alerts when AWS spending approaches a budget. Which AWS service provides this?
options: AWS Budgets — sets cost/usage limits and triggers alerts
No budget alerting available
Cost Explorer for analysis only
CloudWatch alarms for infrastructure metrics only
explain: AWS Budgets sets budget limits and sends SNS notifications when spending approaches thresholds (50%, 90%, 100%). Prevents bill shock.

q: A company compares TCO: on-premises versus AWS. What factors should analysis include?
options: Hardware, software, staffing (salaries), space, power, cooling, disaster recovery, and depreciation
Only hardware cost
Cloud is always cheaper
Only compute costs
explain: TCO includes all on-premises costs: servers, software, IT staff wages, space, power, cooling, backup/DR, and maintenance. AWS typically has lower TCO.

q: What is the difference between an Application Load Balancer (ALB) and a Network Load Balancer (NLB)?
options: ALB is for HTTPS traffic; NLB is for HTTP traffic only
ALB operates at layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) enabling path-based routing; NLB operates at layer 4 (TCP/UDP) for ultra-high performance
NLB is newer and should replace ALB for all use cases
ALB supports Auto Scaling; NLB requires manual scaling
explain: ALB (Layer 7): path-based routing (/api → service A, /web → service B), host-based routing, sticky sessions, HTTP header rules. Best for microservices. NLB (Layer 4): millions of requests/second, static IP, TCP/UDP, ultra-low latency. Best for gaming, IoT.

q: What are EC2 placement groups and when would you use a 'Cluster' placement group?
options: Groups of EC2 instances automatically distributed across AZs for high availability
Cluster placement groups pack instances together in the same AZ for low-latency, high-throughput network performance for HPC
Spread placement groups placing instances on different hardware for fault tolerance
Groups of EC2 instances in different regions for global distribution
explain: Cluster placement groups put instances physically close together in one AZ, enabling 10Gbps+ network performance and low latency. Ideal for HPC, big data, and applications requiring tight node communication. Trade-off: all in one AZ (single failure domain).

q: A company needs 24/7 support with sub-hour response times. Which AWS support plan is appropriate?
options: Support requires additional service purchase
Developer plan for paid support
Basic plan (free, limited)
Business plan (1-hour critical response) or Enterprise plan (15-minute response)
explain: AWS Support: (1) Basic (free, limited), (2) Developer ($29/month), (3) Business ($100+/month, 1-hour response), (4) Enterprise ($15k+/month, 15-min response).

q: A company consolidates multiple AWS accounts. What feature enables consolidated billing?
options: AWS Organizations with consolidated billing and volume discounts
Individual bills for each account
No consolidation available
Cost Explorer alone
explain: AWS Organizations consolidates accounts under a master account, enabling consolidated billing, volume discount purchasing, and unified policies.

q: AWS Trusted Advisor flags 'underutilized EC2 instances.' What does this recommendation suggest?
options: Instances at low utilization can be downsized or terminated to reduce costs
No action needed
Underutilization is unavoidable
All instances must be fully utilized
explain: Trusted Advisor flags instances at low utilization. Downsizing or terminating unused instances directly reduces costs.

q: An organization tracks costs by project/department. How can costs be allocated and tracked?
options: AWS doesn't support cost allocation
Only account-level billing available
Manual spreadsheet tracking only
Cost allocation tags — tag resources; Cost Explorer filters by tags
explain: Cost allocation tags (custom metadata: project, team) are applied to resources. Cost Explorer filters spending by tags, enabling departmental cost visibility and chargeback.

q: A company transfers large amounts of data out of AWS. They see high data transfer costs. How can these be reduced?
options: Data transfer is always expensive
No way to optimize
AWS Direct Connect or reducing transfer frequency/volume
AWS data transfer is free
explain: Data transfer OUT of AWS incurs charges (~$0.02/GB). AWS Direct Connect provides private connectivity reducing costs. Caching via CloudFront also reduces origin transfer.

q: A company runs containerized applications and wants managed orchestration without managing cluster nodes. What AWS service provides this?
options: EC2 instances only
AWS Fargate — serverless containers without managing cluster infrastructure
ECS with EC2 (requires managing instances)
No managed container option
explain: Fargate is serverless container compute: define containers and Fargate handles infrastructure. Pairs with ECS or EKS for orchestration.

q: Which AWS service provides a fully managed GraphQL API that connects to DynamoDB, Lambda, and other data sources?
options: Amazon API Gateway for RESTful API management
AWS AppSync for managed GraphQL APIs with real-time subscriptions and offline data sync
AWS Amplify for full-stack mobile and web application development
Amazon Cognito for API authentication and authorization
explain: AWS AppSync creates managed GraphQL APIs that resolve queries against multiple backends (DynamoDB, Lambda, HTTP, RDS). Supports real-time data subscriptions and offline client synchronization for mobile apps.

q: An application needs to process files uploaded to S3. What is the recommended serverless approach?
options: S3 event notifications triggering Lambda functions to process files
Manual file processing
Polling S3 continuously with EC2
Files cannot trigger automated processing
explain: S3 can emit notifications (SNS, SQS, Lambda) when objects are uploaded, triggering event-driven processing. This is serverless and cost-effective.

q: A company runs batch jobs processing terabytes of data nightly. Which AWS service is most cost-effective?
options: AWS Batch — scales compute automatically for batch processing
Lambda for all batch workloads
Running EC2 instances 24/7
Manual server management
explain: AWS Batch automatically scales compute resources for batch jobs, launching instances when needed and terminating them after completion.

q: A team wants to visualize application performance and correlate it with infrastructure metrics. Which AWS service provides this?
options: CloudTrail for API logging only
No visualization available
AWS CloudWatch — provides metrics, dashboards, and correlates infrastructure with application performance
Route 53 for DNS only
explain: CloudWatch collects metrics from AWS services and custom applications, creating dashboards to visualize performance and correlate issues.

q: A company deploys a microservices application requiring service discovery and load balancing. What does a service mesh or AWS service provide?
options: AWS App Mesh or Elastic Load Balancing for service discovery and traffic distribution
Manual configuration only
Service Mesh only
No AWS service for this
explain: AWS App Mesh and ELB handle service discovery (registering/discovering services) and intelligent traffic distribution across instances.

q: A company reviews their AWS bill and sees charges for data transfer out to the internet. How can they reduce this?
options: Data transfer is unavoidable
AWS Direct Connect reduces transfer costs; caching via CloudFront reduces origin bandwidth
Only paying upfront eliminates transfer costs
No optimization possible
explain: AWS Direct Connect provides private connectivity reducing data transfer costs. CloudFront caching reduces origin bandwidth charges.

q: A startup wants to forecast costs for a new project before launch. What AWS tool helps with this?
options: AWS Trusted Advisor only
No forecasting tools exist
AWS Pricing Calculator — estimates costs based on service configurations
Only historical data is available
explain: AWS Pricing Calculator estimates monthly costs by selecting services and configuring parameters. Provides accurate budgeting before launch.

q: A company uses AWS Organizations for consolidated billing. What additional benefit does Organizations provide?
options: Organizations enables consolidated billing, volume discounts, unified SCPs, and centralized cost visibility
Only consolidates bills
Requires separate AWS accounts elsewhere
No other benefits
explain: AWS Organizations consolidates accounts for billing, enables volume discounts on Reserved Instances, and allows centralized policy management via SCPs.

q: An organization wants to implement chargeback for departmental cloud costs. How should they approach this?
options: Manual spreadsheets only
Cloud costs cannot be allocated by department
Tag resources with cost-center/project; use Cost Explorer to filter and allocate costs to departments
No chargeback mechanism exists
explain: Cost allocation tags (department, project, cost-center) enable filtering in Cost Explorer to allocate costs to departments for chargeback.

q: A company commits $100,000/month in AWS spending. How should they purchase compute to optimize costs?
options: No cost optimization
Spot Instances for all workloads
On-Demand pricing for flexibility
Savings Plans committing to $/hour across instance families and services for 20-66% savings
explain: Savings Plans provide better pricing than On-Demand while maintaining flexibility across instance families and services. Ideal for predictable spending.

q: A startup wants to minimize costs and accept that some workloads may be interrupted. What pricing model is best?
options: Reserved Instances for all
On-Demand pricing only
Pay-as-you-go
Spot Instances for up to 90% discount on spare capacity
explain: Spot Instances provide the deepest discounts (70-90%) on spare AWS capacity but can be interrupted with 2-minute notice. Best for fault-tolerant workloads.

q: A company's AWS costs increased unexpectedly. They need to identify the cause quickly. Which AWS tool provides this?
options: AWS Trusted Advisor for cost optimization only
CloudTrail for API logging only
Manual analysis only
AWS Cost Explorer — shows spending by service, identifies anomalies and cost drivers
explain: AWS Cost Explorer breaks down costs by service, region, account, and usage type. It identifies unexpected cost spikes and anomalies.

q: What is the difference between Standard Reserved Instances and Convertible Reserved Instances?
options: Standard RIs can be sold on the marketplace; Convertible RIs cannot
Convertible RIs are more expensive with no additional flexibility benefits
Standard RIs offer higher discounts (up to 72%) but cannot change attributes; Convertible RIs allow changing instance family/OS/tenancy with slightly lower discounts (up to 66%)
Standard RIs are monthly commitments; Convertible RIs are annual commitments
explain: Standard RIs: highest discount (72%), can sell unused RIs on Marketplace, cannot change instance type/OS/tenancy. Convertible RIs: slightly lower discount (66%), can exchange for different RI attributes during the term. Both offer 1 or 3-year terms.

q: A company wants to prevent budget overruns by alerting teams when costs exceed limits. Which AWS service helps?
options: No budget alerting available
Cost Explorer for analysis only
AWS Budgets — sets cost/usage limits and triggers SNS notifications when approaching thresholds
AWS CloudWatch for infrastructure metrics only
explain: AWS Budgets sets budget limits and sends notifications (SNS) when spending approaches or exceeds thresholds, preventing bill shock.

q: A company evaluates AWS for 5-year planning. They want the lowest possible cost for a long-term commitment. What should they purchase?
options: On-Demand pricing for flexibility
5-year Savings Plans provide deeper discounts than 1-year (if available)
Reserved Instances for maximum savings
Spot Instances for long-term
explain: Reserved Instances offer the deepest discounts for long-term commitments. 3-year RIs are the standard; longer commitments offer better pricing.

q: A company reviews AWS security and wants to understand encryption key management. Which service controls encryption keys?
options: No key management service
IAM only for key management
S3 for key storage
AWS KMS (Key Management Service) — manages encryption keys with IAM policies and audit logging
explain: AWS KMS manages encryption keys used by S3, RDS, EBS, and other services. It provides key creation, rotation, access control, and CloudTrail logging.

q: What is the difference between AWS Budgets alerts and CloudWatch billing alarms?
options: CloudWatch billing alarms are more sophisticated than AWS Budgets
AWS Budgets monitors actual and forecasted costs with granular filtering; CloudWatch billing alarms only trigger when actual charges exceed a threshold
They are identical services with different names in different AWS console locations
AWS Budgets is for enterprises; CloudWatch billing alarms are for individual developers
explain: AWS Budgets: track actual and FORECASTED costs, filter by service/tag/account, set usage budgets, 62-day alert history. CloudWatch billing alarms: simple threshold on actual spend. Budgets is more comprehensive for proactive cost management.

q: A company wants to prevent accidental deletion of critical AWS resources. What service helps enforce this?
options: AWS Config enforces compliance rules; IAM policies with Deny statements prevent specific actions
No prevention mechanism exists
Deletion is always allowed
Security Groups prevent deletion
explain: AWS Config can detect unauthorized deletions. IAM policies with explicit Deny statements prevent deletion of critical resources.

q: A company runs an ecommerce site requiring real-time inventory updates across regions. Which database service provides this?
options: S3 for inventory only
Amazon DynamoDB with global tables for multi-region replication
No real-time option
RDS in single region only
explain: DynamoDB global tables provide multi-region replication with automatic synchronization, enabling low-latency real-time inventory across regions.

q: What is Compute Savings Plans and how does it differ from EC2 Instance Savings Plans?
options: Compute Savings Plans apply only to EC2; EC2 Instance Savings Plans apply to all compute
EC2 Instance Savings Plans are purchased annually; Compute Savings Plans monthly
They have identical discounts but different service coverage
Compute Savings Plans apply to EC2, Fargate, and Lambda across any region/family/OS; EC2 Instance Savings Plans offer higher discounts but are restricted to specific EC2 family and region
explain: Compute Savings Plans (up to 66% off): most flexible — applies to EC2 regardless of family/size/region, plus Fargate and Lambda. EC2 Instance Savings Plans (up to 72% off): higher discount but locked to a specific EC2 instance family and region.

q: A company wants to cache frequently accessed database queries for performance. What AWS service enables this?
options: S3 for caching only
Amazon ElastiCache (Redis, Memcached) — caches data for low-latency access
RDS caching only
No caching service
explain: ElastiCache (Redis or Memcached) provides in-memory caching, reducing database load and improving application response times.

q: Which AWS service helps detect and protect sensitive data like credit card numbers and SSNs stored in S3?
options: Amazon Inspector scanning S3 objects for security vulnerabilities
AWS Config checking S3 bucket encryption settings
Amazon GuardDuty for detecting unauthorized S3 access attempts
Amazon Macie using ML to discover and classify sensitive data in S3
explain: Amazon Macie uses ML to automatically discover sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials, intellectual property) in S3 buckets, classify it, and alert on policy violations. Critical for GDPR, CCPA, and PCI compliance.

q: An application needs a message queue for asynchronous communication. Which AWS service provides this?
options: No queue service available
S3 for messages
Amazon SQS (Simple Queue Service) — managed message queues for decoupled communication
Direct API calls only
explain: Amazon SQS provides managed message queues enabling asynchronous communication between applications and decoupling of components.

q: A company wants to give an EC2 instance permission to write to an S3 bucket securely without storing credentials on the instance. What is the best approach?
options: Use the root account credentials in the application configuration
Store IAM user access keys in a .env file on the EC2 instance
Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance with S3 write permissions — temporary credentials are automatically provided
Create an IAM user and store credentials in Parameter Store on the instance
explain: IAM roles attached to EC2 instances provide temporary credentials via instance metadata (automatically rotated). The application calls AWS SDK/CLI and credentials are provided automatically. No keys to manage, rotate, or accidentally expose.

q: A company wants to stream real-time data (click events, transactions). Which AWS service handles streaming?
options: Amazon Kinesis — collects, processes, and analyzes streaming data at scale
CloudFront for streaming only
No streaming service
S3 for streaming only
explain: Amazon Kinesis captures and processes streaming data (events, transactions) with low latency, enabling real-time analytics.

q: A development team wants to deploy their application with infrastructure as code. Which AWS service provides this?
options: AWS CloudFormation — provisions infrastructure using declarative templates (JSON/YAML)
S3 for storing code only
No infrastructure-as-code option
EC2 manual configuration only
explain: AWS CloudFormation uses templates to declare infrastructure. This enables version control, reproducibility, and easy updates/rollbacks.

q: What is the principle of 'immutable infrastructure' and how does it improve security?
options: Replacing servers rather than patching them in-place — ensures known good state, eliminates configuration drift and persistent access
Locking IAM permissions so they cannot be modified after creation
Using hardware security modules that cannot be tampered with physically
Infrastructure that cannot be modified or scaled after initial deployment
explain: Immutable infrastructure: instead of patching running servers (which accumulate drift), create new AMI with updates and replace instances. Eliminates configuration drift, ensures consistent deployments, and removes persistent access from attackers who may have compromised old instances.

q: Which AWS service provides automated compliance checking against standards like CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark?
options: AWS Security Hub running automated compliance checks against CIS, PCI DSS, and other standards
AWS Trusted Advisor checking basic security best practices
AWS Config rules evaluating specific resource configurations
Amazon Inspector scanning for known CVE vulnerabilities
explain: Security Hub runs automated, continuous compliance checks against standards: CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark, PCI DSS, AWS Foundational Security Best Practices. Provides pass/fail findings with remediation guidance across your accounts.

q: A company wants to monitor their EC2 instances for security events and malware. Which AWS service helps?
options: No security monitoring
Security Groups only
Amazon GuardDuty for threat detection; AWS Inspector for vulnerability scanning
CloudWatch metrics only
explain: GuardDuty detects intrusions and malicious activity. AWS Inspector scans for vulnerabilities in EC2 instances and container images.

q: What is a bastion host (jump box) and what modern AWS service replaces the need for one?
options: A monitoring server collecting logs from all EC2 instances in a VPC
A highly available EC2 instance used as a primary application server
A dedicated hardware server for cryptographic key operations
An internet-accessible instance used as a gateway to access private instances — AWS Systems Manager Session Manager eliminates the need for one
explain: Bastion hosts are EC2 instances in public subnets used to SSH/RDP into private instances. Security risk: exposed public IP, port 22/3389 open. AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides browser-based shell access to private instances via IAM — no bastion, no open ports.

q: Which AWS service provides real-time visibility into your AWS account's adherence to security best practices and compliance standards?
options: AWS Security Hub providing continuous security and compliance posture management
Amazon CloudWatch monitoring operational metrics and performance
Amazon GuardDuty detecting active threats in your account
AWS CloudTrail logging API calls for audit and investigation
explain: Security Hub provides a centralized security and compliance dashboard: aggregates findings from multiple services, runs compliance checks (CIS, PCI, NIST), provides Security Score, and shows findings prioritized by severity with remediation guidance.

q: A company stores sensitive data in S3 and wants to ensure buckets cannot be made public by accident. Which S3 feature prevents public access?
options: S3 bucket policies only
Buckets cannot be protected from public access
S3 encryption only
S3 Block Public Access settings — prevents public ACLs and policies at account and bucket level
explain: S3 Block Public Access settings prevent public ACLs and policies from taking effect, protecting buckets from accidental public exposure. This is a critical security best practice.

q: A multi-account organization wants centralized security policy management and WAF rules across AWS accounts. Which AWS service provides this?
options: CloudFront for CDN rules only
AWS Firewall Manager — manages WAF, Shield, and security groups centrally across accounts
Individual account WAF only
No centralized management
explain: AWS Firewall Manager provides centralized management of AWS WAF, Shield, and security groups across multiple accounts and AWS Organizations.

q: What is a 'confused deputy' attack and how does the aws:SourceAccount condition key prevent it?
options: An attack where a confused attacker gets IAM credentials from the wrong account
When a service acting on your behalf is tricked into acting for another account — aws:SourceAccount ensures the service only acts when called from your account
A man-in-the-middle attack on AWS API calls between services
A social engineering attack targeting AWS account managers
explain: Confused deputy: malicious actor tricks a service (like CloudTrail, Config) with permissions into acting on their behalf. Using aws:SourceAccount and aws:SourceArn condition keys in resource policies ensures the service only acts when called from your specific account.

q: A company deploys AWS WAF with rate-based rules. What attack does this protect against?
options: Encryption issues
No protection from rate-based rules
DDoS attacks and brute force attacks — rate-based rules block IPs making excessive requests
Physical attacks on data centers
explain: AWS WAF rate-based rules block IP addresses making excessive requests within a time period, protecting against DDoS and brute force attacks.

q: A company implements 'zero trust security' in AWS when deploying sensitive applications. What does this security model mean?
options: Only trust AWS services
Trust all users and services by default
Never trust any user or service by default; verify every access request with authentication and least privilege authorization
No authentication needed
explain: Zero trust security assumes no user or service is trustworthy by default. Every access request requires verification via strong authentication and least privilege authorization (multi-factor auth, IAM policies limiting permissions).

q: What are EC2 Auto Scaling policies and what is the difference between target tracking and step scaling?
options: Target tracking scales horizontally; step scaling scales vertically
Auto Scaling policies are for Reserved Instances only; On-Demand uses manual scaling
They are identical policies with different names for different EC2 families
Target tracking maintains a metric at a target value (e.g., 50% CPU); step scaling adds/removes capacity in steps based on alarm breach size
explain: Target tracking: simplest — set CPU to 50%, Auto Scaling adjusts automatically. Step scaling: define scale-out steps (CPU 70%: add 2, CPU 85%: add 4). Scheduled scaling: known traffic patterns. Predictive scaling: ML-based proactive scaling.

q: A company needs persistent block storage for EC2 instances with high performance. Which AWS service provides this?
options: CloudFront for storage
No block storage available
S3 for block storage
Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store) — persistent block storage with multiple volume types (SSD, HDD, provisioned IOPS)
explain: Amazon EBS provides persistent block storage volumes attachable to EC2 instances. Volume types: gp3/gp2 (general purpose), io1/io2 (high performance), st1/sc1 (throughput-optimized/cold).

q: A development team wants to automate building, testing, and deploying applications. What AWS service orchestrates this CI/CD pipeline?
options: AWS CodePipeline — automates build, test, and deployment stages with CodeBuild, CodeDeploy integration
No CI/CD automation available
S3 for storage only
CloudFormation for infrastructure only
explain: AWS CodePipeline orchestrates continuous integration and deployment: CodeCommit (source) → CodeBuild (build/test) → CodeDeploy (deploy). Enables fast, automated releases.

q: A company analyzes customer feedback text to understand sentiment. Which AWS service performs natural language analysis?
options: S3 for text storage only
Amazon Comprehend — uses ML to analyze text for sentiment, entities, and topics
No text analysis available
EC2 for custom analysis
explain: Amazon Comprehend analyzes unstructured text using machine learning: detects sentiment (positive/negative), identifies entities (people, places), and extracts topics.

q: A company wants to build a chatbot for customer service using conversational AI. Which AWS service provides this capability?
options: Amazon Lex — ML service for building conversational chatbots with natural language understanding
CloudFront for chatbot delivery only
RDS for chatbot databases only
No chatbot service available
explain: Amazon Lex powers conversational interfaces using automatic speech recognition (ASR) and natural language understanding (NLU). Used in the Alexa service.

q: A global company operates in multiple languages and wants to automatically translate customer content. Which AWS service provides this?
options: RDS for language storage only
No translation service available
Amazon Translate — translates text between languages using neural machine translation
CloudFront for content distribution only
explain: Amazon Translate translates text between 70+ languages using neural machine translation. Useful for global customer interactions and content localization.

q: A team debugs a microservices application with performance issues across services. Which AWS service visualizes request flows and latency?
options: AWS X-Ray — traces requests through distributed systems, visualizing latency and service dependencies
No tracing service available
CloudWatch metrics only
CloudTrail for API logging only
explain: AWS X-Ray provides distributed tracing: instruments applications to track requests end-to-end, revealing latency in each service and identifying bottlenecks.
